name: Procurement language to mandate platform-neutral fallback protocols
consequences: Direct effect: binds procurement to require platform-neutral fallback; downstream: elevates governance overhead, invites incumbents to contest scope, and potentially slows cycle times, but yields greater resilience and cross-platform interoperability.
options: Draft a policy-backed procurement requirement that explicitly prohibits exclusive reliance on Apple or Google storefronts for core identity services and mandates certified fallback mechanisms across all pilot migrations.
Pilot a multi-vendor certification regime that accredits a platform-neutral identity stack and requires interoperability testing across NSIS and eIDAS reference architectures.
Institute a staged procurement model that allocates preference to platform-neutral solutions tied to resilience milestones and measurable continuity-of-service criteria.
review: Core tension: resilience objectives collide with incumbents' vested interests and certification inertia; weakness: the options may undercount administrative delays and lobbying resistance that slow adoption of platform-neutral procurement language.

name: Develop credible non-production demonstrators using standards-based fallback
consequences: Direct effect: produces credible, standards-based demonstrations of fallback authentication that do not rely on platform-specific signals. Downstream: exposes regulatory, procurement, and certification gaps that must be closed before any real-world deployment, potentially extending timelines and increasing cost.
options: Commission two parallel non-production demonstrators: a mobile-oriented FIDO2/WebAuthn authentication path on GrapheneOS and a browser-based hardware-token fallback, both tested against a simulated identity-provider environment.
Implement a standards-aligned OpenID Connect flow with a portable attestation layer to verify platform-neutral trust signals in a sandboxed environment without live MitID integration.
Create a cross-platform attestation toolkit that standardizes device integrity signals independent of Apple/Google, enabling third-party verifier integration in demonstrators.
review: Core tension: credibility hinges on realistic environments, but the absence of live MitID integration limits transferability; weakness: the options may understate the procurement, security-review, and certification hurdles needed to scale.

name: Policy engagement framework with Digitaliseringsstyrelsen and EU bodies
consequences: Direct effect: establishes formal advocacy channels and requires authorities' responses, aligning policy windows with procurement planning. Downstream: risks slower progress if authorities stall or demand extensive evidence, yet raises legitimacy and future adoption likelihood.
options: Establish a formal advocacy framework that maps stakeholder influence, secures written responses from Digitaliseringsstyrelsen, the MitID operator, and other authorities, and sequences engagements to align procurement timelines with policy windows.
Create a public-private policy lab hosting quarterly briefings with civil society, academia, and policymakers to codify resilience and supplier-diversity requirements.
Publish a policy-anchoring report with concrete AltID requirements and a blueprint for procurement language that Digitaliseringsstyrelsen can adopt in phase two.
review: Core tension: policy momentum may outrun institutional readiness; weakness: the options may overestimate authorities' willingness to adopt concrete language within the planned phase-two window.

name: Risk framing and resilience communications strategy
consequences: Direct effect: reframes platform dependency as a continuity risk and informs procurement and policy decisions. Downstream: could trigger resistance from incumbents who favor status quo and may lead to contested messaging if not balanced with security facts.
options: Develop a resilience-focused risk register and scenario planning that translates platform dependency into procurement and continuity-of-service requirements for AltID.
Launch a public-facing risk communication campaign that frames platform dominance as a single-point-of-failure and pairs it with concrete fallback options and procurement levers.
Coordinate a regulatory-conformance mapping demonstrating alignment with NSIS and eIDAS2 to reduce legal barriers to platform-neutral alternatives.
review: Core tension: messaging may oversimplify security trade-offs and provoke defensive responses; weakness: the options may not spell out concrete operational routes to align security guarantees with procurement constraints.

name: EU interoperability and standards integration
consequences: Direct effect: aligns Danish approach with EU wallet frameworks, boosting interoperability credibility; downstream: may extend decision timelines and require harmonization across different national priorities.
options: Lead a cross-border interoperability test plan with EU Wallet frameworks to validate platform-neutral fallback paths under NSIS and eIDAS2, publicly publishing results and identified gaps.
Sponsor a standards-conformance program with open-source reference implementations to accelerate audits and certification readiness for platform-neutral solutions.
Coordinate with EU working groups to embed Danish requirements into European Digital Identity Wallet materials, ensuring procurement language references platform-neutral fallback criteria.
review: Core tension: EU processes may introduce bureaucratic delays and misaligned incentives; weakness: options may rely on external timelines that push concrete Danish decisions beyond the planned horizon.

name: Physical presence and governance anchoring in Copenhagen
consequences: Direct effect: provides a trusted, security-conscious base for demonstrations and stakeholder coordination; downstream: raises fixed costs and may reduce agility if relocation or scaling is needed.
options: Secure a Copenhagen-based research facility with security-clearance workflows to host demonstrations, stakeholder workshops, and controlled testing environments for identity research.
Establish an on-site liaison program with Digitaliseringsstyrelsen, MitID operator, and university partners to accelerate decision gates and reduce coordination latency.
Develop a mobility plan including temporary field offices in other Danish regions to broaden stakeholder engagement and procurement-readiness conversations beyond Copenhagen.
review: Core tension: physical-base credibility can slow progress; weakness: underestimation of distributed collaboration effectiveness or security overhead for multiple sites.

name: Platform-neutral fallback architecture validation
consequences: Establish a platform-neutral fallback architecture validation through open standards and independent certification. This clarifies technical feasibility and creates a credible basis for procurement language and continuity planning. A potential downstream implication is the need to coordinate across multiple standards bodies and accelerate regulatory alignment, which could stretch timelines.
options: Commission an open-standards validation study with independent auditors to certify a platform-neutral fallback flow and publish the findings for procurement teams.
Undertake a joint technical demonstration across multiple device ecosystems to test end-to-end fallback access using FIDO2/WebAuthn in diverse environments.
Establish a formal certification pathway with a recognized standards body to validate interoperability and trust anchors independent of Apple and Google.
review: Core tension: proving technical feasibility without delaying procurement-ready plans. A key weakness the options miss is underestimating certification timelines and cross-body governance needs, which could push milestones beyond phase two readiness.

name: Procurement language alignment for supplier diversity and continuity guarantees
consequences: Shift procurement toward resilience criteria and supplier-diversity requirements anchored in fallback commitments. Downstream, bid evaluation becomes more complex and negotiation cycles lengthen, but the policy reduces exposure to supplier-concentration risks and improves continuity during disruptions.
options: Draft procurement clauses that require at least two independent trust anchors and a defined fallback path, with periodic audits.
Shift pre-qualification criteria to assess resilience and continuity commitments from all bidders, not just cost and performance.
Include a regulatory trigger when supplier concentration exceeds a defined threshold, enabling rapid mitigation.
review: Core tension centers on balancing cost control with resilience guarantees in procurement. Weakness: the options may underestimate enforcement mechanisms and risk pushback from incumbents, undermining continuity commitments.

name: Civil-society and academic coalition strategy to push platform neutrality
consequences: Coalition-building enhances legitimacy and political salience for platform-neutral reforms. Downstream effects include potential frictions with incumbent operators and longer decision cycles as consensus is sought.
options: Establish a formal coalition agreement with clear advocacy milestones and joint policy briefs distributed to Folketinget and Digitaliseringsstyrelsen.
Organize a series of moderated stakeholder roundtables to surface concerns and build mutual understanding across civil society and government.
Develop a shared risk narrative linking resilience and sovereignty to public value, then disseminate through op-eds and policy briefs.
review: Core tension: coalition credibility versus political practicality. Weakness: risk that coalition influence remains theoretical unless aligned with concrete procurement and policy milestones.

name: EU-aligned interoperability impact assessment and advocacy
consequences: Conduct an EU-aligned interoperability impact assessment to map changes required by AltID and to align with European Digital Identity Wallet standards. Downstream, this demands resources for Brussels engagement and may collide with national procurement pacing and sovereignty considerations.
options: Undertake an EU-facing interoperability impact assessment to map required changes in AltID and align with European Digital Identity Wallet standards.
Submit formal feedback to EU working groups with concrete Danish requirements for platform-neutral fallback mechanisms.
Publish a comparative policy paper outlining how platform-neutral approaches benefit cross-border portability and resilience.
review: Core tension: EU-level alignment pressures against national sovereignty and procurement pacing. Weakness: the options may understate domestic political constraints and Brussels’ timeline effects on Danish decision windows.

name: Hardware-token and browser-based fallback deployment exploration
consequences: Demonstrates non-OS-centric trust and reduces dependence on mobile app stores. This opens hardware procurement and support demands and may elevate user-adoption challenges, but strengthens resilience against platform policies.
options: Pilot a hardware-token-based fallback authentication path integrated with existing AltID workflows.
Develop a browser-based or WebAuthn-first fallback portal that minimizes dependency on app stores.
Validate a GrapheneOS-based device approach with constrained telemetry and open-source components.
review: Core tension: user convenience versus security complexity in non-OS fallbacks. Weakness: the options may overlook accessibility, inclusivity, and training needs across diverse user groups.

name: Independent assurance framework and third-party security review integration
consequences: Establish an external review mechanism to verify risk posture and resilience of platform-neutral concepts. Downstream, this introduces cost and schedule implications, but enhances credibility and accountability with regulators and stakeholders.
options: Establish a standing independent review board to assess platform-neutral proposals and certify risk mitigation plans.
Contract external security experts to perform periodic red-team testing against alternative fallback architectures.
Publish an open risk register and mitigation matrix to enable public accountability and governance oversight.
review: Core tension: independence versus speed and internal alignment. Weakness: external reviews may produce conflicting recommendations that stall decisions if not reconciled with ongoing internal plans.

name: Platform-neutral fallback protocol certification and governance
consequences: Establishes a standards-based fallback path that is not tied to Apple or Google trust anchors. Downstream implications: increases governance, certification, and procurement overhead, potentially delaying near-term rollout and elevating budget risk.
options: Form a cross-domain working group to pilot a standards-based fallback using FIDO2/WebAuthn and OpenID Connect on GrapheneOS devices and certified hardware tokens.
Commission an independent certification lab to validate interoperability and security of the fallback stack against NSIS, eIDAS, and related public-standard requirements.
Incorporate platform-neutral fallback readiness into AltID procurement language with explicit milestones, audit rights, sunset clauses for non-compliant components, and a fallback roadmap.
review: Core tension: a credible fallback must attract vendor engagement and regulatory buy-in, not just demonstrate concepts. Weakness: the options overlook the operational and funding commitments required to sustain governance, certification, and ongoing maintenance.

name: Strategic policy cadence with Digitaliseringsstyrelsen and EU bodies
consequences: Creates a formal policy cadence to unlock procurement changes and shared risk narratives. Downstream: raises coordination demands and runs the risk of engagement fatigue if milestones are not clearly delivered.
options: Establish a formal quarterly policy roundtable with Digitaliseringsstyrelsen, the MitID operator, civil-society groups, and academic researchers to unblock blockers and synchronize milestones.
Publish a compact policy dossier articulating resilience and platform-diversity benefits for Parliament, procurement authorities, and executive agencies to secure political cover.
Launch a structured public consultation on fallback authentication scenarios with clearly defined decision gates, response timelines, and accountability measures.
review: Core tension: policy momentum versus stakeholder coordination; Weakness: options may not secure binding decision-making or track progress.

name: EU interoperability alignment through wallet and NSIS standards
consequences: Aligns national efforts with European wallet goals and portability requirements. Downstream: regulatory processes can be slow and national sovereignty concerns may constrain adoption pace.
options: Submit formal input to European Digital Identity Wallet working groups advocating platform-neutral access and robust fallback certification.
Coordinate cross-border demonstrations with EU member states to test interoperability and fallback under NSIS constraints in realistic environments.
Pursue open-standards development through IETF or W3C activities to codify portable credentials and fallback trust anchors.
review: Core tension: EU alignment can outpace national procurement cycles; Weakness: options may over-commit resources without dedicated funding.

name: Supplier-concentration risk mapping and mitigation program
consequences: Identifies dependency vulnerabilities and informs procurement policies to diversify trust anchors over time. Downstream: can provoke resistance from incumbent operators and require budget reallocations.
options: Publish anonymized risk mappings of MitID dependency across major platforms to inform procurement policy and resilience planning.
Develop diversified authentication pathways, including hardware tokens and browser-based fallbacks, to reduce single-vendor reliance in critical services.
Create a staged contingency budget and reserve funding for fallback components to accelerate procurement when policy alignment occurs.
review: Core tension: diversification versus cost and vendor pushback; Weakness: risk mapping alone may not translate into concrete procurement guardrails.

name: Credible non-production demonstrator program
consequences: Produces credible technical evidence of fallback feasibility without production integration. Downstream: potential misinterpretation as production readiness and misalignment with procurement expectations if timelines slip.
options: Deliver two non-production demonstrators: a mobile proof using FIDO2/WebAuthn on GrapheneOS and a separate browser/token-based fallback with explicit threat models.
Publish a technical dossier detailing interoperability tests, security assumptions, and risk management to enable external validation and EU feedback.
Coordinate controlled demonstrations with select public services in non-production environments to gather real-world insights without exposing live systems.
review: Core tension: demonstration credibility vs. production-readiness risk; Weakness: options may not define concrete acceptance criteria for adoption in procurement.

name: Procurement language integration for platform-neutrality in AltID ecosystem
consequences: Embeds platform-neutrality requirements into procurement documents, creating formal leverage for resilience. Downstream: may slow procurement cycles and invite pushback from incumbent platforms.
options: Draft model contract clauses mandating platform-neutral fallback support across AltID and related identity services, with explicit performance criteria and audit rights.
Attach resilience-based incentives tied to continuity of service and incident response to vendor contracts, avoiding dependence on a single platform.
Define a staged procurement roadmap with explicit milestones for fallback readiness to accelerate adoption whenever political will aligns.
review: Core tension: enforcement of neutrality within existing procurement power; Weakness: options may lack enforcement mechanisms or budget alignment.
